title: Install and use k3s in GroundControl
description: Walk through installing k3s, kubectl, and Helm on the active VPS, then deploy your first project into the cluster.

steps.title: What is k3s?
steps.content:
k3s is a lightweight Kubernetes distribution. In GroundControl it is the **server**: the cluster that runs your containers. kubectl is the **remote** used to talk to it, and Helm is the **app installer** for Kubernetes packages.

You do not need k3s to use kubectl — GroundControl can point kubectl at any cluster — but the default path assumes k3s lives on the active VPS because that is the simplest self-hosted setup.

steps.title: Install k3s on the active VPS
steps.content:
Go to **Services → Install** and click **k3s**. GroundControl will run the official installer on the active host and write a kubeconfig to `/etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml`.

> **Important on Caddy-first hosts:** k3s ships a bundled Traefik exposed as a LoadBalancer. Its ServiceLB (Klipper) pod will claim host ports `:80` and `:443` via iptables DNAT, which breaks Caddy. If you use Caddy as your edge proxy, disable Traefik during install or immediately after. See the *Recovering from a k3s Traefik port hijack* guide for the fix.
steps.aiHint: If this check fails, either k3s is not installed, the kubeconfig path is wrong, or kubectl is not in PATH. Start by verifying 'systemctl status k3s' and that /etc/rancher/k3s/k3s.yaml exists.

steps.title: Install kubectl and Helm
steps.content: In **Services → Install**, click **kubectl** and **Helm**. GroundControl downloads static binaries to `/usr/local/bin`. These tools run on the host and talk to the k3s API.
steps.aiHint: If either command is missing, the install did not place the binary in PATH. Check /usr/local/bin and the active VPS's SystemConfig paths.

steps.title: Install Helm
steps.content: Helm is the Kubernetes package manager. It is optional for basic k3s usage, but many charts and operators use it. Click the button below to install the static binary to `/usr/local/bin/helm`.
steps.aiHint: If helm is not installed, the kubectl step above only installed kubectl. Run this action to add helm.

steps.title: Verify the cluster is reachable
steps.content: Use the terminal or AI assistant to inspect the cluster. You should see one Ready node. Try asking the AI "show me the pods" — it will run `kubectl get pods -A` on the host.
steps.aiHint: A NotReady node usually means the k3s service is not running or the kubelet cannot talk to the API server. Check 'systemctl status k3s' and /var/log/syslog or journalctl.

steps.title: Deploy a project to k3s
steps.content:
1. Go to **Services → Deployments**.
2. Create or edit a deployment.
3. Set **Deploy target** to `k3s`.
4. GroundControl builds a Docker image, imports it into the k3s container runtime, generates Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, Service, Ingress), and applies them.
5. Open **Services → Deployments** to watch status.
steps.aiHint: If pods are in ImagePullBackOff, the image was not imported successfully. Run 'k3s ctr images ls | grep <image-name>' to verify. If CrashLoopBackOff, check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs -n default <pod-name>'.

steps.title: Inspect the cluster from the UI
steps.content: GroundControl exposes read-only Kubernetes APIs at `/api/k8s/namespaces`, `/api/k8s/pods`, `/api/k8s/services`, and `/api/k8s/ingresses`. The topology and AI assistant use these to answer questions like "why is my pod crashing?"
steps.aiHint: If no Service or Ingress appears, the deploy target may not have applied manifests. Check the deployment job output in the UI.
